data.title: Bloom Energy is best performing industrial stock in April
data.description:
The industrial sector has produced some of the most dramatic single-stock moves of the past month, with the top 10 performers delivering gains ranging from roughly 52% to a staggering 137%, a surge largely powered by the intersection of AI-driven power infrastructure demand, defense spending momentum, and a renewed wave of investor interest in energy transition technologies. 

Below is a list of the top 10 performing industrial stocks ranked by one-month price performance. The list includes companies across various industrial sub-sectors such as heavy electrical equipment, electrical components and equipment, construction and engineering, aerospace and defense, and trading companies and distributors, with market capitalizations ranging from approximately $2 billion to over $81 billion.

The list is topped by Bloom Energy Corporation (BE), which posted an exceptional one-month performance of 137.10%. Vicor Corporation (VICR) and American Superconductor Corporation (AMSC) follow as the immediate runners-up, with gains of 89.33% and 66.22%, respectively.

The top performers display a wide range of Quant ratings, reflecting diverse analyst outlooks despite strong recent price action. MYR Group Inc. (MYRG) carries a Strong Buy rating of 4.91, while Eos Energy Enterprises, Inc. (EOSE) holds a Strong Sell rating of 1.13. Other notable entries include Powell Industries, Inc. (POWL) and Custom Truck One Source, Inc. (CTOS), with CTOS earning a Buy rating of 4.24.

Here is the list:

  1. Bloom Energy Corporation (BE), 1 month performance percentage: 137.10%

  2. Vicor Corporation (VICR), 1 month performance percentage: 89.33%

  3. American Superconductor Corporation (AMSC), 1 month performance percentage: 66.22%

  4. Powell Industries, Inc. (POWL), 1 month performance percentage: 65.51%

  5. Legence Corp. (LGN), 1 month performance percentage: 61.37%

  6. York Space Systems, Inc. (YSS), 1 month performance percentage: 57.60%

  7. Custom Truck One Source, Inc. (CTOS), 1 month performance percentage: 56.10%

  8. Intuitive Machines, Inc. (LUNR), 1 month performance percentage: 55.05%

  9. Eos Energy Enterprises, Inc. (EOSE), 1 month performance percentage: 52.45%

  10. MYR Group Inc. (MYRG), 1 month performance percentage: 51.56%

Industrials ETFs: (XLI), (VIS), (FXR), (IYJ), (FIDU), and (RSPN)

data.title: Columbia expects 2026 EPS of $3.55 to $4.00 while maintaining 1% to 3% sales growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Columbia Sportswear Company (COLM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “In the first quarter, we're pleased to have again delivered net sales and profitability exceeding our quarterly guidance, driven by early spring 2026 wholesale shipments and better-than-expected demand in Europe and the U.S. as well as disciplined expense management.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “Our international business, which represents over 40% of our sales, continued to lead our growth, up 16% year-over-year. While our U.S. business remained challenged this quarter and declined 10%...” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “A key product highlight in the quarter was the Bahama shirt... This year, we're celebrating the Bahama's 30th anniversary and expect sales of the Bahama to grow by double-digit percent for the spring '26 season.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “Another PFG highlight on the footwear side is the Dry Tortuga Boot, which saw sales more than triple in Q1.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “We are now expecting a slight improvement... We now expect an approximate 200 basis point unmitigated headwind from tariffs to our full year gross margin outlook.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “As of the date they were terminated, we have paid a total of approximately $80 million in IEEPA tariffs... We have already taken action by submitting our refund claims... We have not yet recognized any benefit of refunds in our financial statements nor have we updated our financial outlook for such refunds.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “We remain steadfast in our commitment to driving shareholder value... including $150 million in share repurchases during the first quarter... exiting the quarter with $535 million in cash and short-term investments and no debt.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “As we look at that order book and we take our advanced orders combined with our anticipation of in-season business for the second half of this year, we do contemplate growth across all geographies led by international and growth across each of our brands.” (Executive VP & CFO Jim Swanson)

Outlook

  • “Based on the information we have today, we are maintaining our full year outlook for net sales growth in the range of 1% to 3%.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “We now expect gross margins of 50.3% to 50.5% or down 20 basis points to flat versus the prior year.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “We are raising our operating margin guidance to 6.7% to 7.5% for the year, leading to diluted earnings per share in the range of $3.55 to $4.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “For the second quarter... we anticipate sales in the range of down 1% to up 1% versus the prior year... result in a loss per share of $0.46 to $0.37.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s full-year EPS outlook of $3.20 to $3.65 and operating margin outlook of 6.2% to 6.9%, management’s current full-year outlook reflects updated tariff assumptions and “the benefit of our accelerated first quarter share repurchase activity.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

Financial Results

  • “Net sales were roughly flat year-over-year at $779 million... Gross margin contracted 20 basis points to 50.7%, driven by 310 basis points in incremental unmitigated tariff costs, partly offset by mitigation actions, including targeted price increases.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “SG&A expenses increased nearly 1%, reflecting higher DTC expenses, partially offset by lower enterprise technology and supply chain personnel expenses, reflecting cost reductions actions which were taken last year.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “Inventories remain healthy and are relatively flat versus the prior year in dollar terms with units down approximately 11% year-over-year.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

Q&A

  • Robert Drbul, BTIG: Asked about remaining fall ’26 bookings, Europe order trends, and disruption risk; CEO & Chairman Boyle answered, “we were pleased that it came in north of that number,” adding, “Europe was good,” while cautioning on “unknowns today about the Middle East conflict and the potential for increased tariffs.”

  • Robert Drbul, BTIG: Asked how refunds would be used; CEO & Chairman Boyle replied, “we clearly haven't contemplated those in our plans for '26,” and said any use would follow “our historical capital allocation plans.”

  • Peter McGoldrick, Stifel: Asked for KPIs on younger-consumer recruitment; CEO & Chairman Boyle said, “the acid test is a larger order book and a bigger revenue,” and pointed to “visits to our website” from recent activations.

  • Peter McGoldrick, Stifel: Asked about price vs. volume and inventory dollars vs. units; CFO Swanson said U.S. targeted increases “have been a high single-digit percent,” and added that wholesale growth implies “less unit volume on that growth.”

  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: Asked about Amaze momentum and partner replication; CEO & Chairman Boyle said Amaze sold “through at very high margins,” and added, “we intend to... make the entire Amaze family a much bigger part of our business.”

  • Tom Nikic, Needham: Pressed on U.S. DTC lag and which channel turns first; CEO & Chairman Boyle said outlets are a “steam valve,” and added, “we expect that digital is going to be the primary way that we expose our brands.”

  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas: Asked about timing shift magnitude and Middle East cancellations; CFO Swanson said, “we beat by around $20 million,” with “roughly half” from timing, “the majority of that was European based,” and added cancellations were “relatively insignificant” to the full-year outlook.

  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas: Asked about oil/input-cost scenario; CFO Swanson said, “it'd be a bit premature for us to provide the exact framing,” while CEO & Chairman Boyle added, “we also have other mitigation efforts, including engineering our products in a different way.”

  • Paul Lejuez, Citi: Asked about demand lost from shortages and fall order book detail; CFO Swanson said the curtailment was “roughly about a $30 million reduction in our planned fall '26 or fall '25 inventory purchases,” and CEO & Chairman Boyle said the U.S. book came in “slightly north of where we thought.”

  • Mitchel Kummetz, Seaport: Asked if early shipments change reorder opportunity; CFO Swanson replied, “Potentially,” but clarified the shift was “relative to what we forecasted and planned for Q1.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, repeatedly probing durability of the U.S. rebound, elasticity, and exogenous risks (tariffs, Middle East, oil), including “why do we think it's sort of taking so long to get that business back to growth?” (Tom Nikic, Needham).

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive but risk-aware, emphasizing execution and caveats, including “the operating environment remains highly dynamic,” and “we are not able to incorporate these risks into our updated 2026 financial outlook.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on early ACCELERATE momentum and initial 2026 setup, the current call added more explicit geopolitical and tariff mechanics, while reiterating confidence with “fortress balance sheet.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Management updated tariffs from “approximately 300 basis points” of unmitigated gross margin impact in the prior full-year outlook to “approximately 200 basis points,” and said refunds have not been recognized in results or outlook. (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • The full-year profitability framework shifted from prior-quarter EPS of $3.20 to $3.65 and operating margin of 6.2% to 6.9% to EPS of $3.55 to $4.00 and operating margin of 6.7% to 7.5%, while keeping net sales growth at 1% to 3%. (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • Analyst questioning moved from order-book completion and marketing efficiency to more granular modeling of timing shifts, DTC recovery sequencing, and input-cost inflation risk. (Multiple analysts)

Risks and Concerns

  • “The operating environment remains highly dynamic... particularly involving tariffs in the U.S. and the conflict in the Middle East.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “This conflict has already triggered order cancellations and forecasted order reductions for certain Middle East distributor markets... the prolonged nature of the conflict poses further risks,” including “late arriving inventory, increased freight and logistics costs and potential order cancellations.” (CEO & Chairman Timothy Boyle)

  • “There's elasticity in our product... There are categories... where we've got more pricing power... and others that less so.” (Executive VP & CFO Jim Swanson)

Final Takeaway

Management said Q1 results came in above quarterly guidance on early wholesale shipments, better-than-expected Europe and U.S. demand, and expense discipline, while the U.S. remained down and international delivered the bulk of growth. Leadership maintained the full-year net sales growth outlook of 1% to 3% and raised full-year operating margin and EPS ranges, citing improved tariff assumptions and accelerated buybacks, while highlighting unmodeled risks from the Middle East conflict, energy-driven input costs, and potential supply chain disruption.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon Mobil in charts: Q1 production of crude oil, NGLs, bitumen, and synthetic oil up 5%; upstream and energy products earnings drop
Serabi Gold plc GAAP EPS of $25.08c, revenue of $51.3M
data.description:
• Serabi Gold plc press release (SBI:CA): Q4 GAAP EPS of $25.08c.
  • Revenue of $51.3M.
  • The Board has announced the Company’s inaugural annual dividend payment during 2025 of 5 pence per share (7 cents per share) in line with the dividend policy adopted in 2025.
  • Cash held at 31 December 2025 of $49.2 million (31 December 2024: $22.2 million).
  • Net cash at 31 December 2025 (after interest bearing loans and lease liabilities) of $42.1 million (31 December 2024: $16.2 million.
  • Gold production for the 2025 full year of 44,169 ounces (2024: 37,520 ounces).
  • EBITDA for the year of $77.9 million (2024: $35.9 million), a 117% improvement year on year.
  • Post-tax profit for the year of $53.9 million (2024: Post-tax profit of $27.8 million), an 94% improvement year on year.
  • Profit per share of 71.18 cents compared with a profit per share of 36.73 cents for the 2024 calendar year.
  • Net cash inflow from operations for the year was $50.6 million after mine development expenditure of $5.3 million, compared to $24.5 million in 2024 after accounting for mine development of $6.3 million.
  • Cash Costs for the full year of $1,437 per ounce (2024: $1,326) and AISC for the full year of $1,816 per ounce (2024: $1,700).
  • The Board announces the 2026 shareholder return policy will be a targeted return of up to 20 - 30% of the Group’s free cash flow to shareholders through dividends or buy-backs.

data.title: Mkango Resources Ltd. reports FY results
data.description:
• Mkango Resources Ltd. press release (MKA:CA): FY  First commissioning runs of recycled NdFeB alloy powder from the commercial scale HPMS vessel at HyProMag’s plant site in Pforzheim, Germany, were announced in April 2026. The site is fully permitted for production of up to 750 tonnes per annum of NdFeB magnets and alloys.
  • Once fully commissioned, the Plant will have a minimum initial capacity of approximately 100 tonnes per annum of NdFeB increasing to up to circa 350 tonnes per annum with multiple shifts. A further expansion to a targeted 750 tonnes per annum is under evaluation.
  • Applying Adamas Intelligence upside forecasts, Songwe's post-tax NPV increases to approximately US$489 million with a nominal IRR of 29%, payback period of 2.9 years from start of full production and post-tax life-of-operations nominal cash flow of $2.04 billion while Puławy's expanded 100% neodymium/praseodymium ("NdPr") separation case rises to a post-tax NPV of approximately US$892 million and nominal IRR of 43%, payback period of 1.89 years from start of full production and post-tax life-of-operations nominal cash flow of $5.58 billion.
  • Cash position of US$3.1 million as at 31 December 2025. Subsequent to the year end, the Company raised net proceeds of £11.7 million (approximately $15.5 million) through the issuance of 37,878,788 common shares of the Company at a price per share of 33 pence (approximately $0.44).
  •

data.title: USA Rare Earth is best performing materials stock in April
data.description:
A diverse group of materials stocks has emerged as one of the market's strongest performing pockets over the past month, with the top 10 names posting gains ranging from 30% to an extraordinary 82.5%, a rally driven by a confluence of geopolitical tailwinds, critical minerals demand, and recovering industrial activity. 

Below is a list of the top 10 performing materials stocks ranked by their one-month price performance. The list spans multiple industries including Diversified Metals and Mining, Aluminum, Specialty Chemicals, and Steel, and accounts for market capitalization and Quant ratings.

The list is topped by USA Rare Earth (USAR), which posted an impressive 82.50% gain over the past month and holds a Quant Rating of 3.46 (Hold). Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) and MP Materials Corp. (MP) follow as the next highest performers, with KALU earning a Strong Buy rating of 4.89 and MP carrying a Hold rating of 2.82.

Other significant companies on the list include Perimeter Solutions, Inc. (PRM), which holds a Strong Buy rating of 4.69, and Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. (EMAT), which is not currently covered by Quant ratings. The list is rounded out by major steel and chemical players like Nucor Corporation (NUE) with a Strong Buy rating of 4.82 and Sensient Technologies Corporation (SXT) with a Hold rating of 2.82.

Here is the list:

  1. USA Rare Earth, Inc. (USAR), 1 month performance percentage: 82.50%

  2. Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU), 1 month performance percentage: 48.67%

  3. MP Materials Corp. (MP), 1 month performance percentage: 44.86%

  4. Perimeter Solutions, Inc. (PRM), 1 month performance percentage: 42.39%

data.title: Sell in May? Hard to make the case with this kind of AI momentum
data.description:
Wall Street is coming off one of its best months in years. The best monthly gain since 2020—to be precise. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) surged over 15% in the span of only 30 days, while the S&P 500 (SP500) climbed 10% and the Dow Jones (DJI) climbed 7%. The advance has been powered by a resurgent AI infrastructure rally, ranging from triple-digit gains for players like Intel (INTC), to strong double-digit performances at AMD (AMD), Sandisk (SNDK), Cisco (CSCO) and Nvidia (NVDA).

SA commentary: "Despite inflationary pressures and high energy prices [given the ongoing conflict in Iran], historical May-July market seasonality and forward EPS upgrades support a continued bullish outlook," SA analyst Mike Zaccardi wrote in a new article. "Information Technology posted a 19% monthly gain, its best in over 20 years, and corporate earnings remain robust, with the S&P 500 pacing for a sixth straight quarter of double-digit YoY EPS growth, keeping valuations in check."

What about "Sell in May and go away"? The famous adage posits that stocks tend to underperform in the six months through October, so investors should convert to cash at the start of May and then buy into a dip later in the fall. The origins of the saying go back quite a while, with reasons ranging from vacation cycles to bonuses, and others noting that the worst market crashes of 1929 and Black Monday in 1987 occurred during this period. Steve Eisman says he sees no issue with market right now

Outlook: Many academic papers and market research have been written on "Sell in May," with breakdowns by stock class or periods of time. While seasonal patterns do exist, and equities could face some increased risk in the summer months, they still tend to go up over the long term despite additional volatility. Staying fully invested could also prove safer than trying to time the market in any given year, and there are countless factors out there for better portfolio decisions, such as earnings, interest rates, the macro landscape and tweets from the White House. Does 'Sell in May' strategy actually work?
